text: Oral contraceptive pill use does not improve exercise performance compared to naturally menstruating women, with the probability of even a small beneficial effect being effectively zero.

Naturally menstruating women may have a slight performance advantage over oral contraceptive users, with a moderate probability of a small effect when comparing OCP withdrawal to the early follicular phase.
population: women using oral contraceptives vs naturally menstruating women

text: Exercise performance remains consistent across the oral contraceptive pill cycle, with no meaningful difference between OCP consumption and withdrawal phases.
Any group-level performance difference between OCP users and non-users is most likely trivial, and current evidence does not support general guidance to start or stop OCP use for performance reasons.
An individualized approach to oral contraceptive use is recommended when exercise performance is a priority, as effects are trivial at the group level but variable across individuals.
There is large between-study variance in the performance effects of oral contraceptives, indicating high individual variability in response to OCP use.
